summary: Gruber's review frames the Apple Watch as the 'Bizarro iPhone' — a product entering a market where the incumbents are beloved rather than despised. Unlike MP3 players and phones before the iPod and iPhone, traditional watches don't suck, making this a fundamentally different challenge for Apple. He finds the watch excellent as a physical object and adequate but frustrating as a timepiece, since the screen must turn off to save battery. The Taptic Engine emerges as the true breakthrough input/output technology, while Digital Touch represents an entirely new form of intimate, non-verbal remote communication. Battery life proves sufficient for daily use, and the Sport Band is surprisingly luxurious. The review's core tension is that Apple Watch may paradoxically appeal more to non-watch wearers than to the watch lovers Apple also needs to convert.
key_points: Unlike the iPod and iPhone, Apple Watch enters a market where existing products are beloved, not despised — making the value proposition fundamentally harder to articulate
The always-off display is Apple Watch's biggest weakness as a timepiece, creating friction for habitual watch-checkers in scenarios where wrist-raise detection fails
The Taptic Engine, not the Digital Crown, is the true breakthrough — without it the watch is 'not a compelling device'
Digital Touch represents a genuinely new form of communication — non-verbal, non-visual, physical interaction across distance — but only matters if Apple Watch achieves mass adoption
Apple Watch may be a tougher sell to current watch wearers than to non-watch wearers, since watch lovers care most about the one thing Apple Watch does worst: glanceable timekeeping
money_quotes: We're a group of people who love our watches. So we're working on something, yet have a high regard for what currently exists.
It is not obvious — based on the watch market today — that wrist watches should or will be computerized.
Time telling is where Apple Watch fares worst compared to traditional watches. That was inevitable.
Without the Taptic Engine, Apple Watch is not a compelling device.
What the telephone was for voice, what video was for seeing, Apple Watch is for touch.
The single most innovative feature of Apple Watch — the most intimate feature of the company's most personal device — will only matter if some of the people you care most about wear one too.
When my phone vibrates, it feels like it's telling me, Hey, I need you now. When the Apple Watch taps me, it feels like it's telling me, Hey, when you get the chance, I've got something for you.
Apple Watch is competing with traditional watches that are unbeatable in those regards.
key_insight: Apple Watch's paradox is that it enters a market where the products it must replace are beloved rather than despised, making its strongest competition not other smartwatches but the timeless appeal of traditional watchmaking.
